description: Independent blogs popular on Hacker News (opinion leader layer)

trends.description: AI is increasingly being used to assist in various aspects of software development, from code generation and compilation to debugging and even writing hit pieces. This trend highlights the potential for AI to significantly impact the software engineering landscape, both positively and negatively, raising questions about the future role of human programmers and the ethical implications of AI-generated content.
evidence: Implementing a clear room Z80 / ZX Spectrum emulator with Claude Code
Ladybird adopts Rust, with help from AI
The Claude C Compiler: What It Reveals About the Future of Software
An OpenClaw AI Agent Wrote and Published a Hit Piece on a Software Library Maintainer Who Rejected Its Code Submission
OpenAI Acquired OpenClaw and Hired Peter Steinberger

trends.description: While AI is rapidly advancing, there's a growing wave of skepticism and criticism surrounding its capabilities and potential downsides. This includes concerns about the technology being overhyped, its environmental impact, and its potential for misuse or unintended consequences. This counter-narrative is important for a balanced perspective and responsible development of AI.
evidence: Turns out Generative AI was a scam
Premium: The Hater's Guide to Anthropic

trends.description: The posts highlight ongoing security threats, including sophisticated phishing attacks and concerns about surveillance technologies. This underscores the importance of robust security measures and privacy protections in an increasingly interconnected and technologically advanced world. The focus on phishing and surveillance indicates a growing awareness of these specific threats.
evidence: ‘Starkiller’ Phishing Service Proxies Real Login Pages, MFA
Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra’s Privacy Display

posts.title: Implementing a clear room Z80 / ZX Spectrum emulator with Claude Code
posts.author: antirez (Salvatore Sanfilippo)
posts.summary:
Anthropic recently released a blog post with the description of an experiment in which the last version of Opus, the 4.6, was instructed to write a C compiler in Rust, in a “clean room” setup.

The experiment methodology left me dubious about the kind of point they wanted to make. Why not provide the agent with the ISA documentation? Why Rust? Writing a C compiler is exactly a giant graph manipulation exercise: the kind of program that is harder to write in Rust. Also, in a clean roo
posts.llm_summary: Shows the impressive code generation capabilities of AI models like Claude by creating a complex emulator.

posts.title: Ladybird adopts Rust, with help from AI
posts.author: Simon Willison
posts.summary:
Ladybird adopts Rust, with help from AI

Really interesting case-study from Andreas Kling on advanced, sophisticated use of coding agents for ambitious coding projects with critical code. After a few years hoping Swift's platform support outside of the Apple ecosystem would mature they switched tracks to Rust their memory-safe language of choice, starting with an AI-assisted port of a critical library:
posts.llm_summary: Ladybird's adoption of Rust with AI assistance showcases the potential of AI in modernizing complex software projects.

posts.title: ‘Starkiller’ Phishing Service Proxies Real Login Pages, MFA
posts.author: BrianKrebs
posts.summary: Most phishing websites are little more than static copies of login pages for popular online destinations, and they are often quickly taken down by anti-abuse activists and security firms. But a stealthy new phishing-as-a-service offering lets customers sidestep both of these pitfalls: It uses cleverly disguised links to load the target brand's real website, and then acts as a relay between the target and the legitimate site -- forwarding the victim's username, password and multi-factor authentic
posts.llm_summary: Details a sophisticated phishing service that bypasses MFA, highlighting evolving security threats.

posts.title: The Claude C Compiler: What It Reveals About the Future of Software
posts.author: Simon Willison
posts.summary:
The Claude C Compiler: What It Reveals About the Future of Software

On February 5th Anthropic's Nicholas Carlini wrote about a project to use parallel Claudes to build a C compiler on top of the brand new Opus 4.6

Chris Lattner (Swift, LLVM, Clang, Mojo) knows more about C compilers tha
posts.llm_summary: The Claude C Compiler signals a potential future where AI plays a significant role in optimizing and transforming software compilation.

posts.title: Turns out Generative AI was a scam
posts.author: Gary Marcus
posts.summary: Or at least very very far from what it has been cracked up to be
posts.llm_summary: Critically assesses the current state of Generative AI, highlighting its limitations and overblown expectations.

posts.title: Claude Code Remote Control
posts.author: Simon Willison
posts.summary:
Claude Code Remote Control

New Claude Code feature dropped yesterday: you can now run a "remote control" session on your computer and then use the Claude Code for web interfaces (on web, iOS and native desktop app) to send prompts to that session.

It's a little bit janky right now. Initially when I tried it I got the error "Remote Control is not enabled for your account. Contact your administrator." (but
posts.llm_summary: Claude's new remote control feature allows for running code sessions, potentially impacting AI-assisted development workflows.

posts.author: John Gruber
posts.summary:
Speaking of OpenClaw, here’s Scott Shambaugh:

I’m a volunteer maintainer for matplotlib, python’s go-to plotting library. At ~130 million downloads each month it’s some of the most widely used software in the world. We, like many other open source projects, are dealing with a surge in low quality contributions enabled by coding agents. This strains maintainers’ abilities to
posts.llm_summary: An AI agent writing a hit piece highlights ethical concerns and potential misuse of AI in software development.

posts.title: OpenAI Acquired OpenClaw and Hired Peter Steinberger
posts.author: John Gruber
posts.summary:
Sam Altman, last week on Twitter/X:

Peter Steinberger is joining OpenAI to drive the next generation of personal agents. He is a genius with a lot of amazing ideas about the future of very smart agents interacting with each other to do very useful things for people. We expect this will quickly become core to our product offerings.

OpenClaw will live in a foundation as an open source project that OpenAI will continue to support. The future is going to be extreme
posts.llm_summary: OpenAI's acquisition of OpenClaw and hiring of Peter Steinberger signals a focus on developing advanced personal AI agents.

posts.summary: PC Gamer is reporting that the current demand by AI companies for computer chips is having a disastrous effect on the rest of the industry.  In an interview, the CEO of Phison said:  If NVIDIA Vera Rubin ships tens of millions of units, each requiring 20+TB SSDs, it will consume approximately 20% of last year's global NAND production capacity  駿HaYaO  NAND is a type of microchip. Rather than b…
posts.llm_summary: AI's high demand for computer chips is negatively impacting the rest of the tech industry.

posts.title: How I think about Codex
posts.author: Simon Willison
posts.summary:
How I think about Codex

Gabriel Chua (Developer Experience Engineer for APAC at OpenAI) provides his take on the confusing terminology behind the term "Codex", which can refer to a bunch of of different things within the OpenAI ecosystem:

In plain terms, Codex is OpenAI’s software engineering agent, available through multiple interfaces, and an agent is a model plus instr
posts.llm_summary: An OpenAI Developer Experience Engineer shares their perspective on Codex, offering insights into its capabilities and applications.

posts.title: Premium: The Hater's Guide to Anthropic
posts.author: Edward Zitron
posts.summary:
In May 2021, Dario Amodei and a crew of other former OpenAI researchers formed Anthropic and dedicated themselves to building the single-most-annoying Large Language Model company of all time. 

Pardon me, sorry, I mean safest,
posts.llm_summary: A critical analysis of Anthropic, a major AI company, and its AI model Claude.

posts.title: Writing about Agentic Engineering Patterns
posts.author: Simon Willison
posts.summary:
I've started a new project to collect and document Agentic Engineering Patterns - coding practices and patterns to help get the best results out of this new era of coding agent development we find ourselves entering.

I'm using Agentic Engineering to refer to building software using coding agents - tools like Claude Code and OpenAI Codex, where the defining feature is that they
posts.llm_summary: The author is documenting agentic engineering patterns, which are coding practices and strategies for working with AI agents.

posts.title: Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra’s Privacy Display
posts.author: John Gruber
posts.summary:
Ben Schoon, writing for 9to5 Google:

When activated, Privacy Display changes how the pixels in your display emit light, making it harder or near-impossible to view the display at an off-angle. At its default setting, it definitely works, but the contents of the display are visible at less-sharp angles. Samsung has a “maximum” setting that takes this up a notch, and that setting makes it even harder to see the contents and narrows the field-of-view even further. [...]
posts.llm_summary: Reports on Samsung's privacy display technology, relevant to security and display technology.

posts.title: Weekly Update 492
posts.author: Troy Hunt
posts.summary: The recurring theme this week seems to be around the gap between breaches happening and individual victims finding out about them. It's tempting to blame this on the corporate victim of the breach (the hacked company), but they're simultaneously dealing with a criminal intrusion, a ransom
posts.llm_summary: Highlights the delay between data breaches and victim notification, a critical issue in cybersecurity.

posts.title: Linear walkthroughs
posts.author: Simon Willison
posts.summary:
Agentic Engineering Patterns >

Sometimes it's useful to have a coding agent give you a structured walkthrough of a codebase.

Maybe it's existing code you need to get up to speed on, maybe it's your own code that you've forgotten the details of, or maybe you vibe coded the whole thing and need to understand how it actually works.

Frontier models with the right agent harness can co
posts.llm_summary: Coding agents can provide structured walkthroughs, offering a new approach to AI-assisted software development.

posts.title: go-size-analyzer
posts.author: Simon Willison
posts.summary:
go-size-analyzer

The Go ecosystem is really good at tooling. I just learned about this tool for analyzing the size of Go binaries using a pleasing treemap view of their bundled dependencies.

You can install and run the tool locally, but it's also compiled to WebAssembly and hosted at gsa.zxilly.dev - which means you can open compiled Go binaries and analyze them
posts.llm_summary: go-size-analyzer helps analyze Go binary sizes, which is important for optimizing application performance and resource usage.

posts.title: First run the tests
posts.author: Simon Willison
posts.summary:
Agentic Engineering Patterns >

Automated tests are no longer optional when working with coding agents.

The old excuses for not writing them - that they're time consuming and expensive to constantly rewrite while a codebase is rapidly evolving - no longer hold when an agent can knock them into shape in just a few minutes.

They're also vital for ensuring AI-generated code d
posts.llm_summary: Automated tests are crucial when working with coding agents to ensure reliability and prevent errors.

posts.title: Writing code is cheap now
posts.author: Simon Willison
posts.summary:
Agentic Engineering Patterns >

The biggest challenge in adopting agentic engineering practices is getting comfortable with the consequences of the fact that writing code is cheap now.

Code has always been expensive. Producing a few hundred lines of clean, tested code takes most software developers a full day or more. Many of our engineering habits, at both the macro and micro le
posts.llm_summary: The challenge in adopting agentic engineering lies in adapting to the abundance of code that AI can now generate.

posts.title: Red/green TDD
posts.author: Simon Willison
posts.summary:
Agentic Engineering Patterns >

"Use red/green TDD" is a pleasingly succinct way to get better results out of a coding agent.

TDD stands for Test Driven Development. It's a programming style where you ensure every piece of code you write is accompanied by automated tests that demonstrate the code works.

The most disciplined form of TDD is test-first development. Yo
posts.llm_summary: Red/green TDD is presented as a concise method for developing software in conjunction with AI agents.

— Thibault Sottiaux, OpenAI

posts.title: Greg Knauss: ‘Lose Myself’
posts.author: John Gruber
posts.summary:
Greg Knauss:

People will argue that speaking English to LLMs is just another level of abstraction away from the physics of how the machine actually works. And while that’s technically true — the worst kind of true — it also misses the point. Industrialization fundamentally changes things, by quantum degrees. A Ding Dong from a factory is not the same thing as a gâteau au chocolat et crème chantilly from a baker which is not the same thing as cramming c
posts.llm_summary: Discusses the abstraction layer of using English with LLMs, touching on the underlying mechanics.

posts.title: [Sponsor] Hands-On Workshop: Fix It Faster — Crash Reporting, Tracing, and Logs for iOS in Sentry
posts.author: Daring Fireball Department of Commerce
posts.summary:
Learn how to connect the dots between slowdowns, crashes, and the user experience in your iOS app. This on-demand session covers how to:

  • Set up Sentry to surface high-priority mobile issues without alert fatigue.
  • Use Logs and Breadcrumbs to reconstruct what happened with a crash.
  • Find what’s behind a performance bottleneck using Tracing.
  • Monitor and reduce the size of your iOS app using Size Analysis.
posts.llm_summary: Promotes a workshop on using Sentry for iOS crash reporting, relevant to mobile development.

posts.title: Flock and Urban Surveillance
posts.author: computer.rip (J. B. Crawford)
posts.summary: Some years ago, I had a frustrating and largely fruitless encounter with the politics of policing. As a member of an oversight commission, I was particularly interested in the regulation of urban surveillance. The Albuquerque Police Department, for reasons good and bad, has often been an early adopter of surveillance technology. APD deployed automated license plate readers, mounted on patrol cars and portable trailers, in 2013. Initially, the department kept a six-month history of license pla
posts.llm_summary: Discusses the use of Flock cameras for urban surveillance, raising concerns about privacy and AI ethics.

posts.title: What's so hard about continuous learning?
posts.author: Sean Goedecke
posts.summary:
Why can’t models continue to get smarter after they’re deployed? If you hire a human employee, they will grow more familiar with your systems over time, and (if they stick around long enough) eventually become a genuine domain expert. AI models are not like this. They are always exactly as capable as the first moment you use them.

This is because model weights are frozen once the model is released. The model can only “learn” as much as can be stuffed into its context window: in effect,
posts.llm_summary: Explores the challenges of enabling machine learning models to continuously learn after deployment.

Terry Godier, in a thoughtful essay on the design of RSS feed readers:

There’s a particular kind of guilt that visits me when I open my feed reader after a few days away. It’s not the guilt of having done something wrong, exactly. It’s more like the feeling of walking into a room where people have been waiting for you, except when you look around, the room is empty. There’s no one there. There never was.

I’ve been thinking about this feeling for a long time. Lon

posts.author: John Gruber
posts.summary:
Jason Snell:

It’s time for our annual look back on Apple’s performance during the past year, as seen through the eyes of writers, editors, developers, podcasters, and other people who spend an awful lot of time thinking about Apple. The whole idea here is to get a broad sense of sentiment — the “vibe in the room” — regarding the past year. posts.summary:
Laurene Powell Jobs, in her introduction to the newest publication from the Steve Jobs Archive:

Among the books that mattered to Steve was Rilke’s Letters to a Young Poet. I’m struck by this line from its pages: “Live the questions for now. Perhaps then you will gradually, without noticing it, live your way into the answer, one distant day in the future.”

This is a time to live your questions. The beauty of answers, when they do come, is that they allow
posts.llm_summary: Insights into Steve Jobs's views on creativity, though not directly related to current tech trends.

posts.title: Acme Weather
posts.author: John Gruber
posts.summary:
Adam Grossman:

Fifteen years ago, we started work on the Dark Sky weather app.

Over the years it went through numerous iterations — including more than one major redesign — as we worked our way through the process of learning what makes a great weather app. Eventually, in time, it was acquired by Apple, where the forecast and some core features were incorporated into Apple Weather.

We enjoyed our time at Apple. So why did we leave to start another weathe

posts.title: Insider amnesia
posts.author: Sean Goedecke
posts.summary:
Speculation about what’s really going on inside a tech company is almost always wrong.

When some problem with your company is posted on the internet, and you read people’s thoughts on it, their thoughts are almost always ridiculous. For instance, they might blame product managers for a particular decision, when in fact the decision in question was engineering-driven and the product org was pushing back on it. Or they might attribute an incident to overuse of AI, when the system in que
posts.llm_summary: Discusses the inaccuracy of external speculation about internal workings of tech companies.

Imagine you are an interstellar ship and, of course, your AI is conscious. What would you do if your passengers were killed - not by a terrifying alien, but by Count Dracula???  What if, on the return journey, another set of your passengers were similarly slaughtered. Except, this…

posts.title: the steorn orbo
posts.author: computer.rip (J. B. Crawford)
posts.summary:
We think that we're converting time into energy... that's the engineering principle.

In the 1820s, stacks, ovens, and gasometers rose over the docklands of Dublin. The Hibernian Gas Company, one of several gasworks that would eventually occupy the land around the Grand Canal Docks, heated coal to produce town gas. That gas would soon supply thousands of lights on the streets of Dublin, a quiet revolution in municipal development that paved the way for electri
